t: Outside the delusion, the patient's general behavior, affect, cognitive functions are relatively preserved; work and relationships can continue as long as they do not touch on delusion content.
One or more delusions persisting for ≥ 1 month.
Schizophrenia failing to meet criterion A (delusion is the sole or dominant symptom; possible when hallucinations are minimal and related to delusional content).
Functional preservation without direct effect of delusion; behavior not bizarre.
If an affective episode is present, it occurs during a small portion of the delusional period.
Exclusion of substance or organic cause.
Permission for bizarre delusions (also removed prohibition in DSM-5-TR); induced delusion (folie à deux) integrated into 6A24.
Subtype qualifiers (erotomanic, grandiose, jealous, persecutory, somatic, mixed, unspecified); 'bizarre type' additional qualifier.
Clinical interview (general and especially delusional content).
Relative/friend interview – premorbid function, onset of delirium.
SCID-5 / MINI; PANSS / BPRS Positive Symptom Subscale.
Toxicology screening; sensor (hearing, vision) test — mandatory in elderly.
Comorbid depression and suicide risk assessment.
Hallucinations, disorganized speech, negative symptoms dominant; significant impairment in functioning.
An affective episode is present during a significant portion of the psychotic symptoms.
Appearance-based; degree of insight is variable, but may be at the level of delusion.
Ego-dystonic obsession primary; distinguished from delusion.
Persistent suspiciousness as a personality trait; no full delusions.
Organic cause — dementia, brain tumor, lupus, encephalitis.
In the context of substance use or withdrawal; remission after elimination.
PANSS / BPRS positive symptom subscale; structured documentation of concrete delusion content.
Hearing and vision test (mandatory in the elderly).
Laboratory: complete blood count, thyroid, B12, liver, glucose, toxicology screening, syphilis, HIV, ANA (lupus), ceruloplasmin (Wilson).
Brain MRI — first episode, atypical clinical picture, elderly patient.
Assessment of suicide and violence risk (C-SSRS, especially in jealousy delusions).
(risperidone 1–3 mg, olanzapine 5–10 mg, aripiprazole 5–15 mg) — first-line. Evidence base is less than for schizophrenia; patient adherence is problematic (due to delusional content).

Historically, erotomania and somatic delusions were considered predominant (Munro A. studies), but in modern practice they are used relatively less frequently compared to atypical presentations (QTc, EPS).
— supportive therapy, building therapeutic alliance; CBT (non-confrontational work with delusional content, exploring alternative explanations).
(especially delusions of jealousy) — bed rest and involuntary treatment within the legal framework.
– hearing devices or vision correction – sometimes have profound effect in elderly paranoid delusions.
NICE CG178 — no separate algorithm for delirium; general psychosis principles.
APA 2021 — antipsychotic first-line; insight and adherence difficulty emphasized.
Munro A. reviews (Cambridge Univ Press 1999) — subtype-specific clinical guidance.
Explore alternative explanations and evidence with patient without openly denying delusion content. Birchwood M., Trower P. studies—effective in schizophrenia and delusional disorder.
Establish long-term trusting relationship with patient; strengthening of adherence.
Standardized assessment of delusion severity and interference degree.
— Suicide risk — particularly in jealousy and persecutory delusions, violence risk additionally assessed.
Prognosis is better than schizophrenia — functional preservation, late onset.
Jealousy and somatic delusions relatively more difficult to treat.
In older adults, improvement with sensory correction is possible.
Monitoring — antipsychotic side effects, delusion severity, violence and suicide risk.
Myth 1: "The patient must be convinced of the falseness of their delusion"
open confrontation disrupts therapeutic alliance and may reinforce delusions; patient develops explanation 'doctor is also against me.'
Birchwood and Trower CBT approaches — a non-confrontational approach allowing the patient to explore alternative explanations themselves is effective.
Myth 2: "Delusional disorder can only be treated with psychotherapy, antipsychotics are unnecessary"
Antipsychotic is first-line pharmacotherapy; psychotherapy alone is insufficient. APA 2021, NICE CG178.
Myth 3: "Erotomania is only a female disorder, absent in males"
Erotomania occurs in both sexes; higher risk of violence reported in male patients (Menzies R.P. et al. Br J Psychiatry 1995).
Myth 4: "Delusional jealousy is a sign of 'great love', not a medical condition"
Othello syndrome is a clinical psychotic condition, high risk of violence and homicide against partner; requires active medical intervention.
Myth 5: "Paranoid delusions in the elderly are 'normal aging'"

In older adults, paranoid delirium is often a marker for sensory deprivation (auditory impairment) or early dementia; it can be treatable. Hearing aids or vision correction sometimes yield significant improvement.
Myth 6: "Exorcism or religious intervention cures delusions"
Psychotic symptoms are neurobiological; antipsychotic treatment delayed.
Myth 7: The delusional patient must be isolated — social contact is harmful
Social isolation, conversely, strengthens psychosis; social support and psychotherapy are recommended.
NICE CG178. Psychosis and schizophrenia in adults. 2014/2019.
APA. Practice Guideline for Schizophrenia, 3rd ed. 2021.
Munro A. Delusional Disorder: Paranoia and Related Illnesses. Cambridge University Press; 1999.
Perälä J. et al. Arch Gen Psychiatry 2007;64(1):19–28.
Menzies R.P., Fedoroff J.P., Green C.M., Isaacson K. Prediction of dangerous behaviour in male erotomania. Br J Psychiatry 1995;166(4):529–536.
Howes O.D., Kapur S. Schizophr Bull 2009;35(3):549–562.
